content: Cancer cachexia is a multifactorial syndrome characterized by ongoing loss of skeletal muscle mass (with or without fat loss) that cannot be fully reversed by conventional nutritional support. Diagnostic criteria: weight loss >5% over 6 months; or BMI <20 with >2% weight loss; or sarcopenia with >2% weight loss. Prevalence: 50-80% of advanced cancer patients, varies by tumor type (pancreatic 80-90%, gastric 70-80%, lung 60-70%, colorectal 50-60%). Pathophysiology: tumor-derived factors (PIF, LMF), systemic inflammation (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1, CRP), metabolic alterations (increased energy expenditure, insulin resistance, lipolysis, proteolysis via ubiquitin-proteasome pathway), anorexia (central appetite suppression), reduced physical activity. Cachexia stages: precachexia (<5% weight loss, anorexia, metabolic changes); cachexia (meets criteria); refractory cachexia (active catabolism, poor performance status, life expectancy <3 months). Impact: reduces treatment tolerance, increases toxicity, impairs quality of life, independent predictor of mortality.
